Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

end of world model.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 20 of about 59 target actions so far.

So each action moves the dot by 2 cells. The valid top-left positions are:

Hmm, that's only 6 positions. To visit all 6, you'd need at most 5 moves. That's way less than 59 target actions.

- Rows: 38, 40 (38+2=40, 40+2=42 but 42+2=44 > 43, so only 38 and 40)
Wait, the container is rows 38-43, so the dot (2x2) can have top-left at rows 38, 39, 40, 41, 42 (since 42+1=43 is the bottom). But if each move is 2 cells, then from row 38, UP would try to go to row 36 (outside), and DOWN would go to row 40. From row 40, DOWN would go to row 42. From [38,10], DOWN → [40,10], DOWN → [42,10]. Let me verify.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check all reachable positions for the green dot
# Current: [38,12]
# Let me map out all positions

# First, move to a known position and then explore
result = action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Green: [{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]")

# Try DOWN again
result = action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Green after DOWN: [{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]")

# Try DOWN again
result = action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Green after 2nd DOWN: [{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
